chapter: Ch 11 — Layer 6: The Bedrock (Primitives, Pairings, Curves, Fields)

phases.pre_rigorous: You have spent two chapters using a polynomial commitment as a sealed envelope: the prover hides a value g^x and later the verifier wants to be sure of some RELATION among the hidden values — say, that one hidden number is the product of two others — without ever opening the envelopes. Ordinary group exponentiation can ADD hidden exponents (g^x · g^y = g^{x+y}), but it cannot MULTIPLY them. A bilinear pairing is the tool that buys you exactly one multiplication on hidden exponents. Lead with the figure: three groups — a blue G₁, a red G₂, and a purple target G_T — and a map e that eats one point from each source group and lands a single element in the target. Drop a point P into G₁ and a point Q into G₂; the pairing produces e(P,Q) over in G_T. Now the magic move: scale the inputs — take aP instead of P, bQ instead of Q — and the target element is raised to the power a·b. Concretely, on a tiny curve over GF(23) with a = 2 and b = 4, the pairing of the scaled points equals the original pairing value raised to the 8th power. You did not learn a or b; you only confirmed that the hidden multiplication a·b happened, sitting safely up in an exponent. (No formal definition yet — first the picture of 'multiply inside the exponent', then the notation.)
phases.rigorous: Now earn it. A bilinear pairing is a map e: G₁ × G₂ → G_T between three groups of the same prime order r, satisfying two properties. (1) Bilinearity: e is linear in each argument separately, e(aP, Q) = e(P, Q)^a and e(P, bQ) = e(P, Q)^b. (2) Non-degeneracy: if P, Q are generators then e(P, Q) ≠ 1, so the map is not trivial. Apply linearity in each slot in turn: e(aP, bQ) = e(P, bQ)^a = (e(P, Q)^b)^a = e(P, Q)^{ab}. That single line is the whole identity. Locate the groups concretely so none of this is mystical: take the supersingular curve E: y² = x³ − x over GF(23). It has an r = 3 torsion subgroup, and its embedding degree is k = 2 — meaning 3 divides 23² − 1 but not 23 − 1, so the full 3-torsion E[3] ≅ ℤ/3 × ℤ/3 and the target group only appear once you pass to the extension field GF(23²). G₁ and G₂ are independent order-3 lines inside E[3]; G_T is the group μ₃ of cube roots of unity inside GF(23²)*. Take P = (2, 12) and Q = (14t+9, 22t+22); the Weil pairing gives e(P, Q) = 19t+15, an element of GF(23²)* of multiplicative order 3 — NOT a curve point. With a = 2, b = 4: e(aP, bQ) = 4t+7 and e(P, Q)^{a·b} = (19t+15)^8 = 4t+7. They are equal; bilinearity holds exactly, with no error term. This destroys the bad intuitions explicitly: (1) the output is NOT a curve point — it lives in the multiplicative group of a field extension, which is why the operation on it is exponentiation; (2) the scalars MULTIPLY in the exponent (8), they do not add (6) — that is what 'linear in EACH argument' forces; (3) you learned a·b only as an exponent, never a or b themselves — recovering a from aP would be the discrete-log problem, which is assumed infeasible.
phases.post_rigorous: Rebuild the intuition on the rigor, both halves at once. 'Multiply inside the hidden exponent' IS the identity e(aP, bQ) = e(P, Q)^{ab}: the picture of two scaled source points feeding one boosted target element is precisely linearity applied in each slot. Pair each picture with its statement — the two source groups G₁, G₂ are the two arguments the map is linear in; the purple target G_T is μ_r, which is why the result is an exponent and not a point; non-degeneracy (e(P, Q) ≠ 1) is what makes the comparison meaningful. Now the book role is obvious: a verifier who wants to check a multiplicative relation among hidden exponents compares two pairing values — this is the single pairing-product verification equation behind KZG's opening check and the Groth16 verifier (a few group elements, one pairing equation). Two boundaries stay sharp. First, a pairing CHECKS a multiplicative relation; it never INVERTS one — it does not recover exponents, so it does not break discrete log; if it did, the whole tower would fall. Second, this only works on pairing-friendly curves: the embedding degree k must be small so G_T lives in a small extension F_{q^k} where e is computable — BN254 and BLS12-381 are engineered for small k, while secp256k1's k is astronomically large, so it has no usable pairing. That single design constraint, plus the attack that sizes these curves (the Extended Tower Number Field Sieve), is why Layer 6 names BLS12-381 specifically.
phases.you_could_have_invented_this: Wanting one multiplication on hidden exponents, demand a map e: G₁ × G₂ → G_T that is linear in each argument: e(aP, Q) = e(P, Q)^a and e(P, bQ) = e(P, Q)^b. Apply both in turn and the identity falls out for free — e(aP, bQ) = e(P, Q)^{ab} — the product in the exponent is forced, not chosen. Add non-degeneracy so the check means something, and you have invented the bilinear pairing: a verifier confirms a·b in the exponent by comparing e(aP, bQ) with e(P, Q)^{ab}, learning the relation without learning the secrets. You derived it; you did not receive it.

heuristic_rigorous_pairs: A pairing lets you multiply two hidden exponents — scale the inputs and the target element is raised to the product.
e: G₁ × G₂ → G_T is bilinear, so e(aP, bQ) = e(P, bQ)^a = (e(P, Q)^b)^a = e(P, Q)^{ab}; the product a·b appears in the target-group exponent.
The pairing's answer is not another curve point — it is a different kind of object you raise to powers.
G_T = μ_r is the group of r-th roots of unity in F_{q^k}^*; e(P, Q) = 19t+15 has multiplicative order 3 in GF(23²)^*, NOT a point on E.
Scaling the inputs multiplies the exponent (it does not add) — a=2, b=4 gives the 8th power, not the 6th.
Linearity holds in EACH argument separately, so the two scalars compose multiplicatively: e(2P, 4Q) = e(P, Q)^{2·4} = e(P, Q)^8 = 4t+7 (Sage-verified), not e(P, Q)^{2+4}.
The pairing checks the hidden multiplication without revealing the secrets — and only some curves can do it cheaply.

Recovering a from aP is the discrete-log problem (assumed hard), so e exposes only a·b in an exponent; and e is computable only when the embedding degree k is small (BN254/BLS12-381), not for secp256k1.

claims.claim: The curve is E: y^2 = x^3 - x over GF(23); it is supersingular and carries an r = 3 torsion subgroup.
The embedding degree for r = 3 is k = 2: the full 3-torsion E[3] ~ Z/3 x Z/3 lives in GF(23^2), not GF(23).
claims.evidence: 3 | 23^2 - 1 = 528 but 3 does NOT divide 23 - 1 = 22, so k = 2. #E(GF(23^2)) = 576 = 3^2 * 64, so r^2 | #E over the extension.

claims.claim: P = (2, 12) generates G1 and Q = (14t+9, 22t+22) generates an independent line G2; both have exact order r = 3.
claims.evidence: Sage (seed 11): P.order() = Q.order() = 3 and P.weil_pairing(Q, 3) != 1, certifying independence (non-degeneracy on this pair).

claims.claim: The pairing value e(P, Q) = 19t + 15 is an order-3 element of G_T = mu_3 in GF(23^2)^*, NOT a curve point.
claims.evidence: Sage: e_PQ = 19*t + 15, e_PQ.multiplicative_order() = 3, an element of GF(23^2)^*.

claims.claim: For a = 2, b = 4 the bilinearity identity holds: e(aP, bQ) = e(P, Q)^{ab}, i.e. 4t+7 = (19t+15)^8.
claims.evidence: Sage: e_aPbQ = 4*t+7; e_PQ^8 = 4*t+7; equality True; manifest bilinear_holds = true.

claims.claim: a*b = 8 and 8 mod r = 2, so e(P,Q)^{ab} = e(P,Q)^2 is a non-trivial power (not the identity).
claims.evidence: 8 mod 3 = 2; since e(P,Q) has order 3, e^8 = e^2 != 1 -- a genuine exercise of bilinearity. Scene shows a*b = 8; mod-3 reduction stated as the order-3 fact, never as a contradiction.

claims.claim: A pairing reveals only the product in the exponent; it does NOT recover a or b (does not break discrete log).
claims.evidence: CHAPTER_BIBLE Ch 11: pairings 'only let you check a multiplicative relation', not recover exponents (that would break discrete-log). Conceptual guardrail; no numeric claim.

scene_value_check.note: Empty list = no drift; every computed value the manim scene displays matches the Sage manifest (recomputed via /opt/conda/envs/manim/bin/python loading SCENE_VALUES against the live manifest).

adversarial_findings.finding: Risk that a*b mod r = 2 makes the displayed exponent 8 look wrong.
adversarial_findings.resolution: Both kept and labeled: scene shows the genuine exponent a*b = 8; 8 mod 3 = 2 is stated as a consequence of order 3 (e^8 = e^2). Sage confirms (19t+15)^8 = (19t+15)^2 = 4t+7. Neither is a typo of the other.

adversarial_findings.finding: Potential conflation: 'pairing reveals a*b' read as 'reveals a and b' (a discrete-log break).
adversarial_findings.resolution: Prose, stuck_points, and a learning objective state the product appears only inside a target-group exponent; recovering a from aP is the discrete-log problem (hard). Sourced to CHAPTER_BIBLE Ch 11 guardrail.

adversarial_findings.finding: Risk the pairing output is described as a curve point (wrong codomain).
adversarial_findings.resolution: Sage: e(P,Q) = 19t+15 has multiplicative_order() = 3 in GF(23^2)^* -- a root of unity, not a curve point. Figure draws G_T as a separate group; prose names it mu_r in F_{q^k}^*.

adversarial_findings.finding: Reproducibility risk from random point selection.
adversarial_findings.resolution: Recipe fixes set_random_seed(11); re-running reproduces P=(2,12), Q=(14t+9,22t+22), e(P,Q)=19t+15, e(aP,bQ)=4t+7 byte-for-byte. SVG 17,923 bytes; mp4 359,669 bytes.

comprehension_set.question: What is a bilinear pairing? Name its domain and codomain, and state the one identity that makes it 'bilinear'.
comprehension_set.answer_key: A non-degenerate map e: G1 x G2 -> G_T between three prime-order groups, linear in each argument, with e(aP, bQ) = e(P, Q)^{ab}. G1, G2 are r-torsion subgroups of an elliptic curve over F_q; G_T is the group of r-th roots of unity mu_r in F_{q^k}^*.
comprehension_set.targets_stuck_point: 'The pairing value is just another point on the same elliptic curve' -- the codomain is a multiplicative group of roots of unity in a field extension, not the curve.
comprehension_set.on_miss_route_to: roots of unity: the r-th roots of unity mu_r in F_{q^k}^*, where the pairing value lives

comprehension_set.question: On E: y^2 = x^3 - x over GF(23) with r = 3 and k = 2, e(P, Q) = 19t+15 (order 3). For a = 2, b = 4, what is e(aP, bQ), and why is it e(P, Q)^8 rather than e(P, Q)^6?
comprehension_set.answer_key: e(aP, bQ) = e(P, Q)^{a*b} = e(P, Q)^8 = (19t+15)^8 = 4t+7. The PRODUCT a*b = 8, not the sum a+b = 6, because the pairing is linear in each argument separately. (Order 3 makes e^8 = e^2, the same element.)
comprehension_set.targets_stuck_point: 'e(aP, bQ) = e(P, Q)^{a+b} -- scalars add in the exponent' -- they multiply; a*b = 8, not a+b = 6.
comprehension_set.on_miss_route_to: cyclic groups: generators, group order, the group operation written additively (point addition) or multiplicatively (exponentiation)

comprehension_set.question: A KZG / Groth16 verifier wants to confirm a multiplicative relation among hidden exponents without learning them. Why is a bilinear pairing the right tool, and what does it crucially NOT let the verifier do?
comprehension_set.answer_key: Encode secrets as exponents (g^x = xP); the pairing turns exponent multiplication into a check on visible elements: e(aP, bQ) = e(P, Q)^{ab}, so the verifier confirms a*b via the single pairing-product equation behind KZG opening and Groth16 verify. It does NOT recover a or b (that is the discrete-log problem, assumed hard) -- it checks a relation, never inverts one. Only small-k pairing-friendly curves (BN254/BLS12-381) make it computable.
comprehension_set.targets_stuck_point: 'A pairing lets you recover the hidden exponents' -- it only exposes the product inside an exponent, never the individual exponents.
comprehension_set.on_miss_route_to: the discrete-logarithm problem: given P and aP, recovering a is infeasible (the bet beneath the whole tower)

comprehension_set.question: You could have invented this. You want an operation that 'multiplies hidden exponents' so a verifier can check a product relation without learning the exponents. What three properties must it have, and how do they force e(aP, bQ) = e(P, Q)^{ab}?
comprehension_set.answer_key: (1) e: G1 x G2 -> G_T (one element from each source group into a third); (2) linear in the first argument: e(aP, Q) = e(P, Q)^a; (3) linear in the second: e(P, bQ) = e(P, Q)^b. Apply (2) then (3): e(aP, bQ) = (e(P, Q)^b)^a = e(P, Q)^{ab} -- the product is forced. Add non-degeneracy (e(P, Q) != 1) and you have the bilinear pairing.
comprehension_set.targets_stuck_point: 'The bilinearity identity is an approximation that holds with high probability' -- it is an EXACT algebraic consequence of linearity in each argument, no error term.
comprehension_set.on_miss_route_to: elliptic-curve groups: points on E form an abelian group; scalar multiplication aP = P+P+...+P
